text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, two bilateral direct inguinal hernias are immediately visible.
The surgeon creates a flap by carefully dissecting and bringing down the peritoneum to access the layers of the abdominal wall.
The surgeon positions the mesh to conceal it from the abdominal cavity, ensuring it is not visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection on the patient's left side, progressing down to the pubic bone, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon reduces the left-sided direct inguinal hernia by pulling it out of the hernia space and freeing it from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the direct inguinal hernia, visibly pulling it out of the hernia space and freeing it from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon reduces a small indirect hernia located around the spermatic cord structures, carefully manipulating the tissue to free it from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon completes the reduction of hernias on the left side and begins creating a surgical flap on the right side.
The surgeon begins reducing the direct inguinal hernia, carefully pulling it out of the direct space to free it from surrounding attachments.
The surgeon shows the completely reduced direct inguinal hernia, with the hernia sac no longer visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces mesh into the abdominal cavity after completing the dissection of hernias on both sides.
The mesh is positioned to cover all hernia spaces on both sides, overlapping in the middle across the pubic bone.
The surgeon places absorbable sutures to fixate the mesh in position, ensuring it remains securely over the hernia spaces.
The sutures are visible, securing the mesh in place to prevent it from sliding as it incorporates into the surrounding tissue.
The final view shows all hernias reduced with the mesh securely in place, spanning across and covering all hernia spaces from side to side.
The surgeon closes the flaps over the mesh, completing the procedure.
The mesh is positioned in such a way that it is completely hidden from the abdominal cavity, ensuring it is not exposed to any underlying bowel.
The surgeon displays the surgical site with the flap completely closed, showing the final arrangement of the mesh covering the hernia spaces.
The surgical field shows the space completely closed off from the abdominal cavity, with the mesh hidden beneath the tissue layers. All sutures have been removed, leaving a smooth surface.
